request.question: What Elton John album included a song that was later covered in 2002 by Tim McGraw?

title: Red Rag Top
Angry All the Time
Watch the Wind Blow By
Tim McGraw (song)
Honky Cat
Emotional Traffic
Mona Lisas and Mad Hatters
Tiny Dancer
Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
One of These Days (Marcus Hummon song)
sentences: "Red Rag Top" is a song written by Jason White and performed by American country music singer Tim McGraw.
It was released in September 2002 as the first single from his album "Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors".
The song peaked at number 5 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart in early 2003 and peaked at number 40 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot 100 chart.
"Angry All the Time" is a song written by Bruce Robison and first recorded on his 1998 album "Wrapped".
It was later covered by Tim McGraw with guest vocalist Faith Hill.
Released in July 2001, McGraw's version was the second single from his "Set This Circus Down" album.
The song reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.
"Watch the Wind Blow By" is a song written by Anders Osborne and Dylan Altman, and performed by American country music singer Tim McGraw.
It was released in October 2003 as the fifth and final single from McGraw's album "Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors".
The song reached the top of the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart on March 20, 2004.
It also peaked at number 32 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
"Tim McGraw" is the debut single and first published song recorded by American singer-songwriter Taylor Swift.
The song was written by Swift and Liz Rose, and produced by Nathan Chapman.
It was released on June 19, 2006 by Big Machine Records as Swift's debut single and the lead single from Swift's eponymous debut album.
Swift wrote "Tim McGraw" during her freshman year of high school, knowing that she and her senior boyfriend would break up at the end of the year when he left for college.
The song was written about all the different things that would remind the subject of Swift and their time spent together, once he departed.
"Tim McGraw" is a musical interconnection of traditional and modern country music.
Lyrically, the track lists items in order to associate a past relationship, one of them being country artist Tim McGraw's music.
"Honky Cat" is a song from the 1972 Elton John album "Honky Château," the album's lead-off track.
Emotional Traffic is the eleventh studio album by American country music artist Tim McGraw.
Originally completed in late 2010, it is McGraw's last studio album released by Curb Records, a label he's been with since his debut album "Tim McGraw" (1993).
It was released on January 24, 2012.
"Mona Lisas and Mad Hatters" is a song from the Elton John album "Honky Château".
The lyrics were written by Bernie Taupin and is his take on New York City after hearing a gun go off near his hotel window during his first visit to the city.
The song's lyrics were partly inspired by Ben E. King's "Spanish Harlem," written by Jerry Leiber and Phil Spector, in which he sings "There is a rose in Spanish Harlem."

In response to this, Taupin writes, Allmusic critic Stewart Mason noted that the song is "less saccharine than many similar Elton John and Bernie Taupin ballads" and praised the "somewhat uncharacteristic emotional directness" of its lyrics.
It was released as the B-side of the "Harmony" U.K. single in 1980.
"Tiny Dancer" is a 1971 song by Elton John with lyrics by Bernie Taupin.
It appears on John's fourth album, "Madman Across the Water", and was released as a single in 1972.
In the U.S. it was certified Gold on 19 May 2005, and Platinum on 19 August 2011 by the Recording Industry Association of America.
In the UK, "Tiny Dancer" has been certified Silver by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI) for sales of 200,000 copies, despite never being released as an official single.
Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.
It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.
Four singles were released.
Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.
The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.
The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000
"One of These Days" is a song co-written and originally recorded by American country music singer Marcus Hummon on his 1995 album "All in Good Time".
It was later covered by Tim McGraw, whose version is the fourth single from his 1997 album "Everywhere".
It peaked at number two in the United States, and number one in Canada.

title: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
Tiny Dancer
Tiny Dancer

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What Elton John album included a song that was later covered in 2002 by Tim McGraw?
hop1_docs: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors | Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.  It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.  Four singles were released.  Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.  The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.  The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000

Let It Go (Tim McGraw album) | Let It Go is the ninth studio album by Tim McGraw.  Released on March 27, 2007, it was his first studio album in two and a half years.  "Let It Go" entered the U.S. "Billboard" 200 at number one with sales of 325,000.  The album has produced seven Top 20 singles on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs charts, including a number one; one of those seven songs was only included on later issues of the album.  Of all McGraw's albums, this one has produced the most singles in his career.

Tim McGraw (album) | Tim McGraw is the first album by American country music artist Tim McGraw, released in 1993.  It includes the singles "What Room Was the Holiday In", "Welcome to the Club", "Two Steppin' Mind", and "Memory Lane", none of which reached the Top 40 on the country charts.  This is the only studio album of McGraw's career not to achieve a music recording sales certification or to enter the Top Country Albums charts.

Angry All the Time | "Angry All the Time" is a song written by Bruce Robison and first recorded on his 1998 album "Wrapped".  It was later covered by Tim McGraw with guest vocalist Faith Hill.  Released in July 2001, McGraw's version was the second single from his "Set This Circus Down" album.  The song reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.
Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida | Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida is a 1999 concept album that contains songs from, but predates the main production of, the 2000 musical "Aida".

Unbroken (Tim McGraw song) | "Unbroken" is a song written by Annie Roboff and Holly Lamar, and recorded by American country music artist Tim McGraw.  It was released in May 2002 as the fourth and final single from his album "Set This Circus Down".  The song reached No. 1 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) charts.

Telluride (song) | "Telluride" is a song originally recorded by Tim McGraw on his 2001 album "Set This Circus Down".  McGraw's version, though never a single, charted twice on Hot Country Songs as an album cut.  It reached No. 52 on its first entry, and then re-entered at No. 59 in 2002.
hop1_titles: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida
response.summary_1:
- Facts from passages:
  - [1] Tim McGraw album: "Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors" — "was released in November 2002" (paraphrase).
  - [1] That album "included a cover of Elton John's 'Tiny Dancer'" (quote short fragment).
- Bridge entity: the song "Tiny Dancer" (connects Tim McGraw's 2002 cover to an Elton John album).
- Missing / ambiguous facts preventing an answer:
  - The passages do NOT state which Elton John album originally included "Tiny Dancer" (missing).
  - Ambiguity: user may mean the original studio album vs a later compilation or greatest-hits release that also contains the song.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval targets (what to fetch and what exact datum to extract):
  1) Fetch the song page "Tiny Dancer (Elton John song)" — extract "original album" / "appeared on" field, original release year, and first album listing (track listing or infobox).
  2) Fetch Elton John album page "Madman Across the Water" — check the track listing and infobox to confirm inclusion of "Tiny Dancer" and the album's release year (if the song page is missing or ambiguous).
  3) If multiple album listings appear (compilations), fetch Elton John's discography or relevant compilation pages (e.g., "Greatest Hits") — extract which release first featured the song to disambiguate "original album" vs compilations.

(No final answer provided here because the supplied passages do not state which Elton John album included "Tiny Dancer".)
response.hop2_query: Which Elton John album included "Tiny Dancer"?
hop2_docs: Tiny Dancer (Hold Me Closer) | "Tiny Dancer (Hold Me Closer)" is a song by Ironik, from his debut album "No Point in Wasting Tears", released on 11 May 2009.  The song heavily samples British singer Elton John's 1972 single "Tiny Dancer".  This is the first single by Ironik to be released without a maxi single.

Tiny Dancer | "Tiny Dancer" is a 1971 song by Elton John with lyrics by Bernie Taupin.  It appears on John's fourth album, "Madman Across the Water", and was released as a single in 1972.  In the U.S. it was certified Gold on 19 May 2005, and Platinum on 19 August 2011 by the Recording Industry Association of America.  In the UK, "Tiny Dancer" has been certified Silver by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI) for sales of 200,000 copies, despite never being released as an official single.

Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors | Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.  It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.  Four singles were released.  Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.  The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.  The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000

Elton John: The Classic Years | Elton John: The Classic Years (known in North America as Elton John: The Remasters) was a remastering series of Sir Elton John's albums, in which some of the albums included bonus tracks.

Elton John (album) | Elton John is the second studio album by English singer-songwriter Elton John, released on 10 April 1970 by DJM Records.  It was released by Uni Records as John's debut album in the United States; many people there assumed it was his first album, as "Empty Sky" was not released in that country until 1975.  "Elton John" includes his breakthrough single "Your Song", and helped to establish his career during what was considered the "singer-songwriter" era of popular music.  In the US, it was certified gold in February 1971 by the Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA).  In the same year, it was nominated for the Grammy Award for Album of the Year.

Rare Masters | Rare Masters is a compilation album by Elton John that was released in 1992.  This compilation album features all A-side and B-sides released by John between 1968 and 1975 that were not included on original studio albums or "Elton John's Greatest Hits Volume II".

Prologue (Elton John album) | Prologue is a grey market album by Elton John featuring music publishing demonstration recordings made in the 1960s.  It features four songs with Linda Peters on vocals, who would later marry musician Richard Thompson.  Elton sings the remaining titles.  The CD is a copy of a promotional 1970 vinyl demo album for producer Joe Boyd's Warlock label.  Only 100 of these original vinyl albums are purported to have been made, of which six are known to exist today.  The CD is a poor quality copy of a damaged vinyl record.  Stylistically, it is very similar to Tumbleweed Connection.  The songs are all written by artists signed to Warlock, including Nick Drake and John Martyn.
hop2_titles: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
response.summary_2: Madman Across the Water. Madman Across the Water includes Elton John's 1971 song "Tiny Dancer." Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors was released in November 2002 and included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer."
